title-group.article-title: Favorable responses to upadacitinib, a JAK1 inhibitor, in long COVID patients with predominant neuropsychiatric symptoms: case reports in 2 autistic patients and one typically developing patient

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/055mfza47grid.412365.70000 0004 0437 9388Department of Pediatrics, Saint Peter’s University Hospital 1, New Brunswick, NJ USA
2https://ror.org/05vt9qd57grid.430387.b0000 0004 1936 8796Department of Pediatrics, Robert Wood Johnson Medical School, Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey, New Brunswick, NJ USA
3New Jersey, Pediatric Neuroscience Institute, Morristown, NJ USA
4https://ror.org/05vt9qd57grid.430387.b0000 0004 1936 8796Department of Neurology, New Jersey Medical School, Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey, Newark, NJ USA

abstract.p: The long-term impact of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) has become evident over the past 3–4 years, with the recognition of post-COVID long-term sequelae, often referred to as long COVID. Neuropsychiatric symptoms are one of the hallmarks of long COVID. In severe cases, it can even present features of encephalopathy. Since some of the neuropsychiatric symptoms associated with long COVID overlap symptoms found in neuropsychiatric disorders, it has been difficult to sort out the effects of long COVID in such subjects. This is especially true in patients diagnosed with autism spectrum disorders (ASD), given their difficult behavioral symptoms and other co-morbid conditions. COVID-19 is thought to affect the onset or progress of encephalopathy symptoms by activation of the immune system through the type 1 interferon (IFN) signaling pathway. In that case, treatment would require an immunomodulating agent that targets such pathways. However, such measures may not be applied to ASD subjects, in whom long COVID may not even be considered as the cause of their symptoms. In this study, we present the beneficial effects of upadacitinib, a JAK (janus kinase) 1 inhibitor, that blocks downstream signaling of type 1 IFNs, on 3 patients, 2 with ASD and one without ASD. In these patients, long COVID was thought to have triggered or aggravated encephalopathy-like symptoms. The beneficial effects of upadacitinib were not only noted by an improvement of their behavioral symptoms but also shown by an improvement of monocyte cytokine profiles (less activated state); peripheral blood monocytes were used as surrogates of microglial cells. These three cases presented highlight a possible use of JAK inhibitors for treating long COVID-associated neuropsychiatric symptoms in both ASD and non-ASD subjects. The presented cases highlight the inherent difficulty of diagnosing long COVID in ASD cases.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a syndrome defined by social-communication delays and other stereotypical behavioral symptoms; its pathogenesis is likely the results of a complex interplay among multiple genetic and environmental factors that vary in presence and degrees among ASD patients [1]. This makes it necessary to develop individualized therapeutic approaches based on each ASD patient’s genetic/epigenetic/environmental conditions. Integrating objective evidence of abnormalities in biochemical pathways, a personalized treatment would target specific pathways of neuronal development, neuronal plasticity, and neuroinflammation.

Neuroinflammation has long been implicated in the pathogenesis of ASD. Microbial infection has been thought to be one of the major triggers for the neuroinflammation observed in ASD, as best demonstrated with the effects of maternal infection during pregnancy [2–5]. The animal model of autism called maternal immune activation (MIA), one of the most rigorously studied animal models of autism, reproduced such clinical findings. In this rodent model, maternal sterile inflammation is induced by injection of endotoxin during the 2nd trimester, leading to ASD-like developmental symptoms in offspring later in life [1, 6]. Such prolonged effects of MIA caused by endotoxin, not associated with specific pathogens, can be explained by the reprogramming of innate immune responses, or innate immune memory (IIM): in IIM, epigenetic changes following potent immune stimuli are implicated in the persistent changes in innate immune responses, either inflammation-prone (trained immunity: TI), or blunted responses (immune tolerance) [7–9]. Such changes not only occur in effector cells, but also in progenitors, causing lasting effects, and are also known to be transmissible to offspring, as seen in the MIA model [10, 11].

In the state of a complete lack of pre-existing immunity, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-Cov-2) likely induces potent inflammation-skewed IIM. This assumption is supported by the presence of post-acute sequelae of COVID-19, or long COVID, often presented with prominent neuropsychiatric symptoms [12, 13]. Patients suffering from mild COVID-19 in the acute stage, can still suffer from severe, persistent neuropsychiatric symptoms with evidence of inflammation affecting the innate immune cells in the brain [14, 15]. As stated above, a role of neuroinflammation has been suspected in some ASD subjects who often exhibit fluctuating neuropsychiatric symptoms following microbial infection [16]. In these ASD subjects, dysregulated IIM may already exist [10]. If such ASD subjects suffer from long COVID, it may be very difficult to sort out long-COVID-induced changes in their neuropsychiatric profiles. Impaired communication skills in ASD subjects further compound the inherent difficulties in diagnostic certainty. To sort out COVID-induced effects in ASD patients, it will be helpful to carefully monitor behavioral changes in both ASD and non-ASD subjects with long COVID, in comparison with immune parameters that likely reflect COVID induced changes. Indeed, we have shown worsening behavioral symptoms in parallel with more inflammation skewed innate immune responses in long COVID ASD cases [17].

Apart from the difficulty in diagnosing long COVID in ASD subjects, treating long COVID is also challenging [18]. If we can identify evidence as to which signaling pathways are activated by SARS-Cov-2, one of the formidable treatment measures will be the use of immunomodulating agents that inhibit such signaling pathways. SARS-Cov-2 is known to activate the immune system through the type 1 IFN pathway [19]. The down-stream Type 1 IFN pathway is mediated by JAK1 and therefore, JAK1 inhibitors can be applicable for controlling long COVID symptoms, if immune workup results indicate such a possibility [20]. We present three cases of long COVID patients, two ASD subjects and one non-ASD subject. All the patients presented with predominant neuropsychiatric symptoms with evidence of activation patterns of innate immune cells through the type 1 IFN pathway. These patients revealed favorable responses to upadacitinib, a JAK1 inhibitor, illustrating the possible utility of upadacitinib as a treatment option for neuropsychiatric symptoms associated with long COVID.

sec.title: Questionnaires
sec.p: Clinical features of neuropsychiatric symptoms were evaluated with the use of the Aberrant Behavior Check list (ABC), a validated questionnaire developed for ASD subjects. The ABC has been used as the standard measure for evaluation of aberrant behaviors in various clinical trials, permitting us to compare our clinical data with previously published data.

sec.title: Monocyte cytokine profile
sec.p: To assess changes in innate immune responses caused by COVID-19, we measured monocyte cytokine profiles in the 2 subjects enrolled in the study. Peripheral blood monocytes (PB Mo) were purified from the peripheral blood mononuclear cells with the use of immunoaffinity column (Miltenyi Biotec North America, Gaithersburg, MD). Then monocytes (5 × 105 cells/ml) were cultured overnight without a stimulus or with stimuli of innate immunity as described previously [21]. In this study, we used lipopolysaccharide (LPS), zymosan, CL097, and candida heat extract (as a source of ß-glucan) as stimuli [21]. Concentrations of monocyte cytokines (IL-1ß, IL-6, IL-10, IL-12p40, IL-23, TNF-α, sTNFRII, TGF-ß, CCL2, and IFN-α) in the culture supernatants were measured by ELISA (enzyme linked immunosorbent assay). Reagents of ELISA assay was obtained from Becton Dickinson (BD), Franklin Lakes, NJ for IL1ß, IL-6, IL-10, IL-12p40, TNF-α, and CCL2 ELISA and R & D, Minneapolis, MN for sTNFRII, TGF-ß, and IFN-α ELISA. Kynurenine ELISA was obtained from Express Biotech International Company, Frederick, MD.

sec.title: Case 1 ASD subject
sec.p: An18-year-old Caucasian male presented in the Pediatric Allergy/Immunology Clinic in April 2020, secondary to clusters of generalized tonic-clonic seizures and worsening behavioral symptoms following an upper respiratory infection (URI). He was diagnosed with pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified (PDD-NOS) at 3 years of age, and his diagnosis was changed to autism spectrum disorder (ASD) at 4 years of age. Following various intervention measures, he became mainstreamed, with an aid, in the 6th grade. He suffered from generalized tonic-clonic seizures twice in 2017; the anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam) was started. He remained seizure-free for the next 2 years and levetiracetam was discontinued. Video electroencephalogram (vEEG) monitoring was normal after discontinuation of levetiracetam in 2019. Toward the end of 2019, he had marked exacerbation of ASD behavioral symptoms that included self-injurious behaviors (SIBs), aggression, and sudden rage symptoms. This was followed by clusters of tonic-clonic seizures that occurred in February 2020. He was initially suspected of having autoimmune encephalitis (AE). However, his AE workup [lumbar puncture (LP), vEEG, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), AE autoantibody screening in the serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)] was unremarkable. Seizures were controlled with the use of divalproex which was later switched to lamotrigine and lacosamide; he is currently only on lacosamide.

Over the next 4 years, he repeatedly contracted SARS-Cov-2, and his neuropsychiatric symptoms and seizures got worse each time. He was documented to have COVID-19 at least six times, as was confirmed by the presence of SARS-Cov-2 nucleocapsid antibodies initially, and then PCR (polymerase chain reaction) or antigen (Ag) testing. His main source of repeated SARS-Cov-2 exposure was from his family members. Retrospectively, it was suspected the onset of his AE-like symptoms and seizure clusters in early 2020 was triggered by SARS-Cov-2 exposure.

He was tried on various immunomodulating agents under the diagnosis of seronegative AE or PANS (pediatric acute-onset neuropsychiatric syndrome) by multiple providers. He had temporary responses to oral steroid, minimal responses to antibiotics (azithromycin, amoxicillin, cefdinir, etc.), or mycophenolate. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) was sought for a trial, but was not approved by his insurance, owing to his negative AE workup. Celecoxib and minocycline provided temporary improvement. After realizing the impact on COVID-19 on his clinical course, colchicine and then anakinra were added to his treatment regimen. He revealed favorable responses to colchicine, with temporary improvement of his difficult behavioral symptoms. Anakinra was tried mainly for controlling seizures. However, his favorable responses to colchicine/anakinra were lost with another bout of COVID-19, despite treatment with nirmatrelvir/ritomavic (Paxlovid®). Since SARS-Cov-2 activates the immune system through the type 1 IFN/Jak1 signaling pathway, he was then started on upadacitinib (15 mg ER 1 tablet once daily) along with colchicine. He revealed the most favorable clinical responses to colchicine plus upadacitinib; within 1–2 weeks, his parents reported noticeable improvement of his difficult behavioral symptoms (hyperactivity, irritability, aggression, and emotional outburst) that prompted AE workup. His favorable responses to upadacitinib paralleled the improvement of his ABC subscale scores three months after the start of upadacitinib (Fig. 1). However, he suffered from a tonic-clonic seizure when the lamotrigine dose was lowered. He recovered quickly with an increase in dose of colchicine from 0.6 mg twice daily to 0.6 mg three time daily and his lamotrigine was successfully discontinued. Additionally, he was later taken off upadacitinib, without worsening behavioral symptoms.

sec.title: Case 2 ASD subject
sec.p: A 32-year-old Caucasian female presented to our clinic in 2018. She was diagnosed with ASD (level II to III) around 2 years of age and has been tried on various intervention measures. One of the authors was previously involved in her care at another institution for treating her refractory seizure disorder. Namely, she suffered from tonic-clonic seizures in her teens and started on IVIg, which helped control her seizures. IVIg was also continued due to her history of mastoiditis and diffuse cholesteatoma, requiring repeated surgical interventions and intravenous antibiotics. She was suspected of having an underlying autoimmune condition and eventually diagnosed with anti-phospholipid syndrome (APS). Persistently high IgG antibody titers against ß2 glycoprotein supported APS diagnosis; this autoantibody became detectable in her late teens. Then she was treated with various immunomodulating agents against APS including rituximab which led to stabilization of her APS until the end of 2017. IVIg was switched to subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SQIg) for reducing post-infusion side effects and secondary to increasingly difficult IV access.

In the end of 2017, she suffered from severe headache and marked exacerbation of her ASD behavioral symptoms. Her acute exacerbation was proceeded by sinusitis and a retropharyngeal abscess. Subsequent neurological workup revealed high intracranial pressure (ICP), and she was diagnosed with the CNS flare-up of APS. She was initially treated with diuretic (acetazolamide), IV steroid bolus, oral steroid, and removal of CSF fluid. These measures partially eased her symptoms. Subsequent vEEG still revealed evidence of encephalopathy which prompted us to resume rituximab treatment (325 mg/m2 2 doses 2 weeks apart every 6 months). Rituximab helped control her symptoms, but recurrence of her neuropsychiatric symptoms became evident by the time of the next course. By the 7th course, her responses to rituximab waned. With her CNS flare-ups, according to her mother, she lost some of her once acquired cognitive functioning. After the 7th course of rituximab, she suffered from herpes zoster infection which was treated with oral valacyclovir for several weeks. Therefore, the maintenance medication was switched to baricitinib (4 mg/day), a JAK2 inhibitor, that initially brought her APS symptoms under control. Baricitinib was tried secondary to the report of favorable responses in SLE patients and her diagnosis of anti-phospholipid syndrome [22].

Then in summer of 2022, she contracted COVID-19. Her acute COVID-19 symptoms were mild with the treatment of mirmatrelvir/ritonavic. Colchicine was added to the regimen after completion of anti-viral treatment to control post-COVID-vascular inflammation [17, 23, 24]. However, after recovering from the acute stage of COVID-19, her neuropsychiatric symptoms became noticeably worse, becoming less responsive to baricitinib. Considering the action of SARS-Cov-2 on type 1 IFN pathway, baricitinib was switched to upadacitinib (30 mg ER tablet, 1 tablet/day) which blocks JAK1, a down-stream gate-keeper enzyme, mediating type 1 IFN signaling pathway [20]. A high dose of upadacitinib was used considering her diagnosis of APS and her history of severe CNS flare-up with increase in ICP, as described above. Within 1–2 weeks after starting upadacitinib, noticeable improvement of her behavioral symptoms was recognized by her family members. This is reflected in the improvement of ABC subscale scores (Fig. 2). Six months after starting upadacitinib, she also appeared to have regained some of her cognitive functioning that was lost after the CNS flare-ups of APS in late 2017. Namely, her speech improved greatly in parallel with an increase in her vocabulary and being more conversational. Her mother also reports that she is relearning her language skills and doing better with reading and comprehension.

sec.title: Case 3 non-ASD subject
sec.p: A 15-year-old Caucasian female presented to the Pediatric Allergy/Immunology Clinic for evaluation of possible use of immunomodulating agents for persistent tic disorder and developmental regression in Oct. 2023. She was diagnosed with neonatal epilepsy and ectopic atrial tachycardia at birth, but she responded well to pharmacological interventions (propranolol, vitamin B6, and phenobarbital) with resolution of seizures. Genetic workup for seizure disorders was negative, and the above-described medications were slowly tapered off by 2 years of age. She was developing typically until 14 years of age.

In the fall of 2022, mild neuropsychiatric symptoms following an URI occurred, which was then followed by the onset of vocal tics. After suffering from another bout of URI at the end of 2022, her motor and vocal tics became substantially worse. By January 2023, there was also noticeable loss of cognitive functioning. vEEG was negative both in the fall of 2022 and in February 2023. She was suspected to have AE and underwent extensive AE workup through the neurology service (MRI, vEEG, LP, AE autoantibody screening in the serum and CSF), however, results were all negative. Extensive blood work revealed no diagnostic clues, despite the steady deterioration of her cognitive functioning, and worsening motor/verbal tics, along with the onset of dysautonomic symptoms resembling POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome), significant joint/muscle ache, and headache. She started on IVIg (1 g/kg/dose every month) which was then eventually increased to 2 g/kg/dose every month. A high dose (HD) IVIg helped control her symptoms by about 50% according to her parents, but the debilitating clinical conditions described above persisted. She was placed on home instructions in the late fall of 2023. Retrospectively, she had SARS-Cov-2 exposure toward the end of 2022, based on the history of COVID-19 in her family members, but she was not tested as she only had minimal URI symptoms at that time. This assumption is supported by the results of her innate immune responses tested initially: these results, in our experience, were consistent to those seen in long COVID subjects (Fig. 4). In addition, she also experienced a temporary recurrence and worsening of her neuropsychiatric symptoms when she was confirmed to have the bout of COVID-19 (PCR positive) in July 2024. However, this flare-up was much milder due to the use of mirmatrelvir/ritonavic.

After being seen in the clinic, she first started on minocycline. She appeared to have had favorable responses to minocycline with partial improvement of cognitive functioning (better short-term memory, etc.). Then in March 2024, she developed seizure clusters and developmental regression (speech loss, loss of short-term memory) along with onset of hallucinations when she was started on metronidazole by another provider. Metronidazole was switched to nitazoxanide, and she was started on colchicine for providing additional anti-inflammatory effects. Unfortunately, she became unresponsive to the HD IVIg, minocycline, colchicine, and neurotropic medications (mainly sertraline). Considering her innate immune responses which indicated activation of type 1 IFN pathway, upadacitinib was started first at 15 mg/day and then increased to 30 mg/day. Noticeable improvement became evident within just one week after increasing the upadacitib dose to 30 mg/day. Overall, her condition stabilized, and her tic symptoms gradually subsided for the next 4–5 months. However, she is still suffering from impaired cognitive functioning: difficult to follow regular high school curriculum and was placed in the 5-year program with tutoring. Improvement of her neuropsychiatric symptoms are reflected in change in her ABC scales (Fig. 3).

sec.title: Changes in behavioral symptoms by ABC
sec.p: In all 3 cases presented, we were able to assess changes in behavioral symptoms before and after upadacitinib treatment with the use of the ABC. This questionnaire was originally developed for evaluating behavioral symptoms in ASD subjects. However, we found behavioral symptoms that are listed by the questionnaires have been noted in the non-ASD long COVID patients in our clinical practice. Therefore, we used the same questionnaires for the evaluation of long COVID neuropsychiatric symptoms in both ASD and non-ASD subjects. As shown in Figs. 1, 2 and 3, noticeable improvement was observed in all the ABC subscale scores in the 3 presented cases. Noticeable improvement in the symptoms of irritability, lethargy, and hyperactivity was noticed within 1–2 weeks in all three cases. In case 3, improvement of ABC subscale stereotypy was most notable, although as expected, in this non-ASD subject, all ABC subscale scores are substantially low as compared to Cases 1 and 2 who are diagnosed with ASD.

caption.p: Changes in ABC subscale scores before and 3 months after starting upadacitinib in the Case 1
Changes in ABC subscale scores before and 6 mo after starting upadacitinib in the Case 2
Changes in ABC subscale scores before and 6 weeks after starting upadacitinib in the Case 3

sec.title: Changes in cytokine profiles by PB mo
sec.p: Changes in monocyte cytokine profiles before and after upadacitinib treatment are available for cases 1 and 3; they were enrolled in the current study protocol for checking changes in innate immune responses in long COVID subjects. The results obtained in both Cases 1 and 3 revealed a decrease in spontaneous production of inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-1ß) and the counter regulatory cytokine (IL-10) (Fig. 4 Panels A, B, C). Increase in IL-10 was not surprising, since patients suffering from autoinflammatory syndromes tend to reveal persistently high spontaneous IL-10 production, but such IL-10 production is often suppressed with stimuli of innate immunity as shown in our presented cases (Fig. 4 panel D). Production of TNF-α and IL-1ß by PB Mo was less after starting upadacitinib when compared to the levels prior to the start of this medication in both Cases 1 and 3. When the type 1 IFN-α pathway is activated, generated cytokines by innate immune cells are known to activate indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO), shifting tryptophan metabolism from serotonin to kynurenine. We also observed declining serum levels of kynurenine in both Cases 1 and 3 after starting upadacitinib (Fig. 5).

caption.p: Changes in production of TNF-α, IL-1ß, and IL-10 (pg/ml) by peripheral blood monocytes under cultures witout stimuli (medium only) and under the CL097 stimulated cultures before and after the upadacitinib treatment in Case 1 and Case 3
Changes in the serum levels of Kynurenine (pg/ml) before and after the upadacitinib treatment in Cases 1 and 3

sec.title: Discussion and conclusions
sec.p: The three cases presented, all of whom had severe encephalopathy-like symptoms, revealed favorable responses to upadacitinib, an oral JAK1 inhibitor. In these three cases, post-COVID sequelae or long COVID severely affected their neuropsychiatric symptoms and cognitive functioning. Given the fact that SARS-Cov-2 activates the immune system, mainly through the type 1 IFN-JAK1 signaling pathway, JAK1 inhibitors that inhibit downstream of type 1 IFN-signaling are expected to exert therapeutic effects as shown in the case presented. The presented cases indicate the feasibility of the use of JAK1 inhibitors as treatment options for those with neuropsychiatric symptoms associated with long COVID.

The impact of COVID-19 has been enormous, affecting almost every aspect of our society. As the COVID pandemic progressed, it became clear that unusually high numbers of patients suffered from lingering symptoms after recovering from the acute phase of COVID-19. These lingering symptoms are referred to as long COVID and affect multiple organs by recent definition of long COVID [25], indicating a complex underlying pathogenesis [12, 19, 26]. Neuropsychiatric symptoms (brain fog, anxiety/OCD, cognitive impairment, etc.) are one of the most frequently observed sequelae [18, 27]. Such prolonged effects on the CNS are unlikely to be associated with chronic infection, considering generally rapid clearance of SARS-Cov-2 from the body. Instead, these sequelae are more likely the results of the altered immune responses caused by potent immune activation by SARS-CoV-2, partly through epigenetic and metabolic changes of innate immunity (or changes in IIM) [7, 10, 26, 28]. An initial immune defense against SARS-CoV-2 is exerted by type 1 IFNs, rendering potent inflammatory responses [29] that lead to the inflammation-skewed IIM or TI [7]. This results in excessive innate immune responses that keep recurring in response to subsequent immune insults, even with immune stimuli unrelated to SARS-CoV-2.

Pathological consequences of excessive activation of type 1 IFN pathways are best exhibited in patients suffering from genetic conditions causing excessive type 1 IFN production or activation of type 1 IFN pathways referred to as interferonopathies [30]. These patients frequently present with neuropsychiatric symptoms (e.g., anxiety, OCD, etc.) and impaired cognitive functioning like post exertion neuroimmune exhaustion [30]. These symptoms exhibited by patients with interferonopathies resemble the neuropsychiatric symptoms and neurological deficits detected in long COVID patients [14, 31]. In animal models of long COVID, persistent neuroinflammation has been shown to affect multiple neuronal cells, including microglial cells, indicating a role of neuroinflammation mediated by the CNS innate immune cells [31, 32]. AE-like symptoms associated with COVID-19 have also been reported [33, 34]. Although a role of neuroinflammation has been implicated in the pathogenesis of ASD, COVID-induced neuroinflammation appears distinct, compared to those previously suspected of triggering neuroinflammation in ASD, as reviewed by one of the authors [35].

Since the onset of the COVID pandemic, we have been treating numerous ASD and non-ASD subjects suffering from long COVID. In our experience, they often present with predominant neuropsychiatric symptoms. The long COVID symptoms seem to vary over time, affected by various, unrelated immune insults, as expected in the state of dysregulated TI. However, we soon realized that in the ASD population, long COVID is more difficult to diagnose. Their limited expressive language and pre-existing difficult ASD behaviors make it challenging to appreciate the effects of long COVID in ASD subjects. Although the pathological mechanisms of long COVID are still not fully understood, some immunomodulating agents that affect downstream signaling of the type 1 IFN pathway appear to provide favorable effects in long COVID patients in both our and others’ observation [17, 20]. Activation of the type 1 IFN signaling pathway and resultant Th17 cell activation have been implicated in the pathogenesis of various autoimmune conditions, as seen in patients diagnosed with interferonopathies [30].

Key down-stream signaling molecules in this pathway are Janus kinases (JAK). In fact, JAK inhibitors have been used for controlling the above-described autoimmune/autoinflammatory conditions triggered by type 1 IFN signaling [36, 37]. JAKs act as signal transducers and 4 mammalian members are identified: JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, and TYK2 [20, 38]. All JAKs, except for JAK3, are expressed ubiquitously, and play a crucial role in the JAK-STAT (signal transducers and activators of the transcription) pathways [38]. Dysregulated activation of the JAK-STAT pathway will lead to chronic inflammatory conditions, implicated in pathogenesis of autoimmune, allergic, and autoinflammatory conditions [39]. In patients with interferonopathies, type 1 IFN signaling involving JAK1 and possibly JAK2 are proposed to be therapeutic targets [30]. Likewise, cytokines noted to be up-regulated in patients with severe COVID-19 utilize the JAK/STAT pathways and levels of these cytokines are reported to be positively associated with the disease outcome/mortality of COVID-19 [40]. Therefore, JAK inhibitors have been used for treating COVID-19 [20].

Baricitinib, which predominantly blocks JAK2, has been used for treating severe COVID-19 cases requiring hospitalization. In a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial for severe COVID-19 patients, baricitinib was reported to have caused less adverse reactions when used as an adjunctive therapy to remdesivir and dexamethasone [41]. Multiple open-label studies have also reported beneficial effects of baricitinib for treating severe COVID-19 [42]. With the increase in reports of the favorable effects of baricitinib, FDA issued the EUA (emergency use authorization) for its use as an adjunct treatment with remdesivir for the treatment of hospitalized COVID-19 patients older than 2 years of age. Newly available predominant JAK1inhibitors (upadacitinib and abrocitinib) are expected to be more effective for controlling type 1 IFN induced immune activation, since JAK1 is the down-stream gate keeper enzyme of type 1 IFN signaling [43, 44].

In all 3 cases presented (Figs. 1, 2 and 3), noticeable improvement of behavioral symptoms was observed after starting upadacitinib. In both ASD subjects (Cases 1 and 2), an improvement of their behavioral symptoms was shown by the improved ABC scores. However, the core ASD behavioral symptoms remained (Figs. 1 and 2), as opposed to almost complete resolution of behavioral symptoms in non-ASD case (Case 3) (Fig. 3). In Case 2, the patient reported regaining cognitive functioning once lost after an APS CNS flare-up that had occurred almost six years prior to the start of upadacitinib. This is a very encouraging finding, indicating the possibility of regaining cognitive functioning even several years after the loss of functioning if the neuroinflammation is properly treated.

Case, a non-ASD subject with long COVID, revealed moderately high scores in ABC subscale stereotypy, which was normalized after upadacitinib treatment, indicating long COVID can temporarily induce distinct neuropsychiatric symptoms.

In Cases 1 and 3, we were also able to check cytokine production profiles by PB Mo, which were used as surrogates of microglial cells, since PB Mo are known to migrate to the brain upon immune activation and serve as bone marrow-derived microglial cells, exerting pro-inflammatory responses [45]. When patients suffer from COVID-19, resultant activation of innate immune cells like monocytes are thought to be through the type 1 IFN pathway, leading to an increase in the production of both inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1ß, and IL-6) and those enhancing Th17 responses (IL-12p40 and IL-23). We did observe such cytokine production patterns by PB Mo in our long COVID patients, both with and without ASD (unpublished observation), as seen in these 2 cases presented. Jak1 inhibitors like upadacitinib are expected to inhibit the production of such monocyte cytokines, since they block the downstream of type 1 IFN signaling pathway [20]. We also observed a decrease in production of these cytokines, most notably in production of IL-1ß and TNF-α in both cases (Fig. 4). This is most notable in the spontaneous production of IL-1ß and TNF-α. In addition, when monocytes are activated in vivo, a compensatory increase in production of counter-regulatory cytokines, like IL-10, is expected to occur as in patients diagnosed with autoinflammatory syndrome: Cases 1 and 3 also revealed high spontaneous production of IL-10 which was reduced after the start of upadacitinib. When monocytes are already activated, under the cultures with stimuli of innate immunity like CL097, IL-10 production was suppressed. However, such suppression was less evident after updacitnib treatment (Fig. 4-D). Likewise, the serum kynurenine level, which is augmented by IL-1ß and other inflammatory cytokines, has been reported to be higher in long COVID patients [46, 47]. Indeed, we observed a decrease in serum kynurenine levels in Cases 1 and 3 after Upadacitinib treatment (Fig. 5). These findings indicate that improvement seen in neuropsychiatric symptoms appear paralleled with changes in immune parameters implicated with long COVID.

These three patients presented were already treated with colchicine for controlling post-COVID vascular inflammation, so that clinical improvement may be more evident with concurrent use of colchicine and upadacitinib. It is also of note that these 3 cases were previously treated with multiple immunomodulating agents typically used for controlling post-infectious encephalopathy including IVIg, steroid, mycophenolate, rituximab, anti-seizure mediations, etc., without satisfactory benefits. These results indicate the importance of targeting specific signaling pathways associated with Sars-Cov-2 activation for treating long COVID.

In summary, the three cases of long COVID both with and without ASD revealed lingering encephalopathy-like symptoms, not responding to various intervention measures. However, noticeable improvement in their neuropsychiatric symptoms and cognitive functions were achieved with the addition of upadacitinib to the treatment regimen. These cases indicate that Jak1 inhibitors can offer an additional treatment option for treating long COVID patients with or without ASD if immune workup results indicate activation of type 1 IFN pathway.

def.p: Pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The author was thankful for Dr. Lisa Huguenin for critically reviewing the manuscript.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: H. J. prepared the study protocol, analyzed/interpreted the patient data regarding immunological data, and monitored their responses to immunomodulating agents. She also is primarily responsible for preparation of this manuscript. J. K. conducted neurological workup and analysis of the neurology data and helped edit the manuscript. L. G. conducted assays of monocyte cytokine profiles and analysis of cytokine data.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was partly funded by Jonty Foundation, St. Paul, MN, O’Sullivan Foundation, Princeton, NJ, and the Brain Foundation, Pleasanton, CA.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: Two subjects (Case 1 and Case 3) were enrolled in the research study that addresses changes in innate immune responses in long COVID patients. This study was approved by the institutional review board at our institution (Pro # 22:42) and the signed informed consent forms were obtained for these subjects.

notes.title: Consent for publication
notes.p: The signed informed consent for publication of the presented cases were obtained. The consent obtainment processes were detailed below:

1) The consent form (Pro#22.42) obtained for Cases 1 and 3 include agreement of publication of the obtained data without disclosing personal health information. However, since Case 3 is a minor, we also obtained the signed consent form for publication from her parents with the use of institutional consent form for publication.

2) Case 2 was not enrolled in the study protocol, since she is above the age limit of the study (older than 30 years of age). For Case 2, we obtained the signed consent form for publication with the use of institutional consent form: both her legal guardian and the patient (Case 3) signed the consent form.
There are no identifiable personal information or images included in this publication.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
